senses.definition: Able to read but not interested in reading.
examples: Many aliterate teenagers spend hours online but never pick up a book.
The school is trying to turn aliterate students into enthusiastic readers.

senses.definition: A person who can read but rarely chooses to read for pleasure or information.
examples: Schools fear they are producing aliterates who prefer screens to books.
An aliterate may own many books but never actually read them.

senses.definition: Plural of aliterate: people who can read but rarely choose to read.
examples: Teachers are worried about the growing number of aliterates in their classes.
In a digital age, aliterates often rely on short videos instead of written texts.

senses.definition: Third person singular of aliterate: acts in a way that shows no interest in reading.
examples: He aliterates by choosing TV over books every time.
When a child consistently aliterates, parents may need to model a love of reading.

senses.definition: The middle part of an insect’s body, including the thorax and the first segment of the abdomen, especially in ants and wasps.
examples: The wasp’s alitrunk is narrow and clearly separated from the abdomen.
Researchers measured the length of the alitrunk to compare different ant species.

senses.definition: Not involving an official liturgy or set religious service.
examples: Some days in the church calendar are aliturgic and have no special service.
The group chose an aliturgic form of worship without a fixed order of prayers.

senses.definition: Not following or using a set liturgy in worship.
examples: Some Christian communities prefer an aliturgical style of prayer.
The service was deliberately aliturgical, with no fixed prayers or responses.

senses.definition: The state or quality of being a particular kind of thing, often used in academic or philosophical language.
examples: The article discussed the ality of gender in modern society.
Scholars sometimes create new words ending in "‑ality" to express complex ideas.

senses.definition: From another place or from an outside source, especially used about evidence in law.
examples: The facts of the case must be proved aliunde, not just from the contract itself.
The court accepted evidence aliunde to explain the unclear terms in the will.

senses.definition: From a source other than a particular document, statement, or piece of evidence.
examples: That he was present was proven aliunde the written confession.
The judge allowed facts to be shown aliunde the contract.

senses.definition: Living, not dead.
examples: All the puppies are alive and healthy.
She was badly hurt in the accident, but she is alive.

senses.definition: Full of activity, energy, or feeling.
examples: The city is alive at night with music and lights.
The old house seemed alive with memories.

senses.definition: Knowing and strongly aware of something.
examples: We must be alive to the risks of climate change.
Managers should be alive to their staff’s concerns.

senses.definition: The quality of being alive, energetic, or full of life.
examples: You can feel the aliveness of the forest in spring.
Meditation helped her reconnect with a sense of aliveness.

senses.definition: Plural of aliveness, used rarely to talk about different kinds or experiences of being full of life.
examples: The book explores the many alivenesses we can feel in body and mind.
Different cultures celebrate different alivenesses through music and dance.

senses.definition: A rare, nonstandard plural form of alive, referring to living people or things.
examples: The poet spoke of the alives and the deads of the city.
He tried to give voices to all the forgotten alives of history.

senses.definition: Relating to a bond or obligation that lasts for the lifetime of a person, especially in older legal or church language.
examples: An alivincular vow was considered unbreakable during the person’s life.
Some alivincular agreements lost their force after legal reforms.

senses.definition: A given name used for people of any gender.
examples: Alix moved to Paris to study design.
I met an artist named Alix at the gallery.

senses.definition: Immigration of Jews to Israel, or one such act of moving there.
examples: Her grandparents made aliya in the 1950s.
The government offers support to families planning aliya.

senses.definition: The act of Jews moving to live in Israel, or the general idea of Jewish immigration to Israel.
examples: After years abroad, they decided to make aliyah.
Aliyah from different countries has shaped modern Israeli society.

senses.definition: An honor in a Jewish religious service when a person is called up to read from the Torah.
examples: He received an aliyah at his cousin’s bar mitzvah.
Family members were given aliyot during the holiday service.

senses.definition: A likely erroneous or fused form combining "aliyah" and its plural "aliyahs"; not used in standard English.
examples: The string "aliyahaliyahs" appears to be a typo in the list.
Writers should avoid nonstandard forms like "aliyahaliyahs".

senses.definition: Plural of aliyah: acts of Jews moving to live in Israel, or separate periods of such immigration.
examples: Different aliyahs have brought new cultures to Israel.
The book describes the early aliyahs from Eastern Europe.

senses.definition: Plural of aliyah: honors in Jewish worship when different people are called up to the Torah.
examples: Family members shared the aliyahs during the ceremony.
On holidays, more aliyahs are added to the service.

senses.definition: Variant plural spelling of aliya or aliyah, meaning Jewish immigrations to Israel or honors of being called to the Torah.
examples: The museum’s exhibit focuses on early aliyas from North Africa.
During the festival service, several aliyas were given to visitors.

senses.definition: Ashkenazi or Yiddish‑influenced plural form of aliyah, especially for the Torah honors in Jewish services.
examples: At the bar mitzvah, family members took several aliyos.
The rabbi assigned the aliyos before the holiday service.

senses.definition: Hebrew plural form of aliyah, used in English for Torah honors or periods of Jewish immigration to Israel.
examples: The aliyot were carefully divided among the guests.
Historians speak of several major aliyot in the twentieth century.

senses.definition: Less common transliteration of the Hebrew plural of aliyah, used for Torah honors or waves of Jewish immigration to Israel.
examples: Older texts sometimes refer to the first and second aliyoth.
The rabbi assigned the aliyoth before the morning service.

senses.definition: A salt or chemical compound derived from alizarin, used especially in dyeing.
examples: The chemist prepared an alizarate to improve the fabric dye.
Early textile factories experimented with different alizarates.

senses.definition: A variant or related form of alizarin, used as a red dye.
examples: The fabric was colored with alizari to give it a deep red shade.
Alizari was once widely used in traditional textile production.

senses.definition: A red organic dye originally obtained from the madder plant and now usually made synthetically.
examples: Artists once relied on alizarin for rich red paints.
The lab synthesized alizarin for use in textile dyes.

senses.definition: A spelling variant of alizarin, a red dye used in textiles and paints.
examples: The old recipe mentions alizarine as the main coloring agent.
Manufacturers replaced natural alizarine with synthetic dyes.

senses.definition: Plural of alizarine, referring to different forms or uses of this red dye.
examples: The company developed several alizarines for different fabrics.
Scientists tested various alizarines to find the most stable color.

senses.definition: Plural of alizarin, used for different types or preparations of this dye.
examples: They experimented with several alizarins to achieve the desired shade.
Modern alizarins are more light‑resistant than older ones.

senses.definition: A rare plural or variant form related to alizari or alizarin dyes.
examples: Historical records mention different alizaris used in carpet making.
The workshop stored its alizaris in small glass jars.

senses.definition: In medieval Spain, a self‑governing community of Jews or Muslims under Christian rule.
examples: The Jewish aljama had its own leaders and courts.
Historians study the records of the aljama to understand daily life.

senses.definition: Written or expressed in the style or language of an aljamiado text, especially Romance language in Arabic script.
examples: The aljamado manuscript preserves old Spanish in Arabic letters.
Aljamado literature offers a unique view of Muslim Spain.

senses.definition: A Romance language, especially Spanish, written in Arabic script by Muslim communities in medieval Spain.
examples: Scholars study aljamia to understand the daily language of Moriscos.
The poem was preserved in aljamia in a family collection.

senses.definition: Written in Spanish or another Romance language using Arabic script, especially in medieval Muslim Spain.
examples: Aljamiado texts are valuable sources for historians.
The sermon survived only in an aljamiado version.

senses.definition: A text written in a Romance language using Arabic script.
examples: The library holds several aljamiados from the sixteenth century.
An aljamiado in the collection describes local customs.

senses.definition: A variant form referring to aljamia, the use of Romance language written in Arabic script.
examples: The glossary explains several terms in aljamiah.
Aljamiah materials are rare but historically important.

senses.definition: A long outer garment or coat in some Spanish‑speaking or historical contexts.
examples: The traveler wrapped his aljoba tightly against the wind.
In the painting, the merchant wears a dark aljoba.

senses.definition: A wide, shallow bowl or basin, especially used for washing in Spanish‑speaking contexts.
examples: She poured water into the aljofaina to wash her hands.
An old metal aljofaina stood beside the bed.

senses.definition: Short form used in chemistry for alkyl, alkali, or alcohol, depending on context.
examples: In the formula, "alk" stands for an alkyl group.
The label used "alk" to abbreviate alcohol content.

senses.definition: A supposed universal solvent in old alchemy that could dissolve any substance.
examples: Alchemists searched in vain for the mythical alkahest.
Stories about alkahest reflect the desire for total knowledge.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the power of alkahest, the universal solvent in alchemy.
examples: The story describes an alkahestic liquid with magical powers.
He dreamed of discovering an alkahestic substance that could dissolve all metals.

senses.definition: An extremely rare or obsolete form meaning alkahestic, related to the idea of a universal solvent.
examples: The treatise mentions an alkahestica spirit in a single passage.
Alkahestica references are mainly of historical interest.

senses.definition: Having the nature of alkahest or acting like a universal solvent.
examples: He imagined an alkahestical fluid that could clean any stain.
The legend speaks of an alkahestical power hidden in the stone.

senses.definition: Plural of alkahest, referring to imagined universal solvents in alchemical writings.
examples: Different alchemists described their own alkahests.
Stories of powerful alkahests appear in many old manuscripts.

senses.definition: The bright star at the end of the handle of the Big Dipper, in the constellation Ursa Major.
examples: Alkaid is one of the seven main stars of the Big Dipper.
The guide pointed out Alkaid to the group of stargazers.

senses.definition: A chemical compound that is both an alkali and an amide, or an amide of an alkali.
examples: The researcher isolated an alkalamide from the reaction mixture.
Alkalamides can show interesting biological activity.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which the blood is more alkaline than normal.
examples: Severe vomiting can lead to alkalemia.
The patient showed signs of alkalemia on the blood test.

senses.definition: The state of being slightly alkaline or becoming more alkaline.
examples: The solution showed a slight alkalescence after heating.
Changes in soil alkalescence can affect plant growth.

senses.definition: Plural of alkalescence, describing several states or degrees of becoming alkaline.
examples: Different alkalescences were observed in the series of solutions.
The researcher compared the alkalescences of various samples.

senses.definition: Another plural form of alkalescence, referring to several instances of becoming more alkaline.
examples: The study noted slight alkalescencies during the reaction.
Multiple alkalescencies occurred as the mixture was titrated.

senses.definition: The tendency of a solution or substance to become alkaline.
examples: The alkalescency of the water rose after the treatment.
Monitoring alkalescency is important in this industrial process.

senses.definition: Becoming alkaline or slightly alkaline.
examples: The mixture turned alkalescent after adding the base.
Some mineral waters are naturally alkalescent.

senses.definition: A substance, usually a base, that reacts with acids and has a pH greater than 7.
examples: Soap is made using an alkali and fat.
Strong alkali can burn your skin, so you must wear gloves.
